# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. Strong Female Lead with NO Romance

**Score:** 125
**Body:** Can you suggest me some movies that have strong female leads and no romantic plotline? 

The genre doesn't really matter, although I enjoy thought provoking movies. It also shouldn't involve sexual violence. 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Prey
2. Alien.
  - Alien, Aliens, and Terminator 2.
3. Pacific Rim. I was impressed that even though the male and female leads have to have such a close working relationship throughout the movie, they never spoil it by getting romantically involved
4. The Descent
5. Colossal. Go in blind.
6. The Pelican Brief
7. Fargo, Furiosa, Whatever Happened to Baby Jane, Denial, Temple Grandin
8. She's not a lead but Rachel McAdams in Spotlight (2015) is a good example of a female character that does not involve romance at all with any of the male characters. She is just smart and good at her job and stands just fine on her own. 
9. A Few Good Men.  If Demi Moore is basically a lead alongside Tom Cruise and Jack Nicholson. Her character didn't hook up with Cruise's on purpose. 

## 2. The Worst Movie

**Score:** 32
**Body:** Hey! I do this thing with my friend where we watch shit movies and we’re trying to find the worst one ever, so please share the worst film you’ve ever seen, also if you know what streaming service it’s on in the UK that would very helpful!

Edit- Thank you to everyone who made suggestions! I’ll have a look at these when I get a chance!

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Manos the Hands of Fate, the MST3K version is a gem!
2. "The Room" is probably an expected answer because it's known to be one of the worst movies, but it's all I can think of. 
3. Ape (1976) aka Attack of the Giant Horny Gorilla
4. Birdemic: Shock and Terror ✌️🤪
5. Nothing But Trouble (1991) — don’t know about the streaming availability in the UK, but for the sake of humankind I can’t help hoping it’s… nowhere.
6. Troll 2. There’s even a whole documentary about how it’s at cult status now with all the people who consider the Best worst movie to watch. The documentary is called “Best Worst Movie” and is Free on YouTube right now too. Makes a really great companion piece to watch after you see the movie   
EDIT: both the full movie of Troll 2 AND the documentary are FREE on YouTube!
7. Sex and the City 2
8. "things" the 1989 Canadian movie. Cover has a guy with a mullet, a mustache and a big power drill. 
9. Jesus Christ Vampire Hunter is on Tubi
10. The Asylum is a film studio that pumps out B-grade films, maybe one a week? Their most famous film was Sharknado. But there are thousands of unsung heros, and more coming out every month.

There is also a documentary called Mockbuster, following a director trying to make an Asylum film, which is straight up comedy. They film a feature in 6 days. Fraught with disaster.

## 3. Lesbian Rom-Com?

**Score:** 26
**Body:** I’m looking for a rom-com movie or series that has either a lesbian/bi woman romance of some kind that’s one of the main focuses or draws of the film/series - but the catch is that I don’t want Heartstopper or Heated Rivalry, because they’re more like coming of age and sport dramas imo, and also because they’re gay men-focused (duh).

Also I don’t want any tragedies or films with the ‘bury your gays’ issue; give me some wholesome sapphic love.

Thankyou!

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. But I'm a Cheerleader!
2. I'd definitely recommend *Imagine Me & You* (2005). It's sweet, funny, and one of my favorite sapphic rom-coms with a genuinely satisfying ending. Also *Crush* (2022) if you're looking for something lighter and more modern
3. Happiest Season
4. Fried Green Tomatoes has tense/emotional moments (the first 30 minutes has two occasions of death/injury, not the lesbians tho) and it isn't bury your gays

On the other hand, it's not an explicitly sapphic movie because film studios 

Edit: apparently it is a bury your gays movie and I misremembered
5. D.E.B.S.!
6. The kids are alright.  Comedy/drama about a lesbian couple and their kids. Great cast. Won several awards.
7. But I’m a Cheerleader

Saving Face

Bottoms

Booksmart (sort of)

Crush (it’s coming of age and sports, I’m sorry 😂 )

Also check out some of the old KindaTv youtube series (Carmilla, All For One, Barbelle, ect for some fun options)
8. Happiest Season (2020) - it’s a Xmas movie though
9. I haven't seen it in a long time but Kissing Jessica Stein
10. Better than Chocolate - I was obsessed with this movie in high school but so sad to see a 46% on rotten tomatoes! 😭 It’s available for rent on YouTube, Prime, Google Play, etc, but I don’t think it’s free anywhere. 

The series “The L Word” Is pretty soapy, lots of hot sex scenes. It’s on paramount + for sure

## 4. Looking for movies similar to german series Dark

**Score:** 24
**Body:** As a huge Dark fan, I really love watching apocalyptic thriller movies and mind bending sci-fi. I'm open to any recommendations, thank you all

Edit: additionally, time travelling

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Dark matter on apple TV. Its more parallel universe than time travel, but its great. 
2. Time travel list

The tomorrow war, back to the future, Time crimes, Palm Springs, beyond the infinite two minuites, primer, saftey not guranteed, somewhere in time, midnight in Paris, the triangle, frequently asked questions about time travel, edge of tomorrow, predestination, synchronic
3. TV series:

*Devs*

*Tales from the Loop*

*Black Mirror*

Movies:

*Primer*

*Sorry to Bother You*

*Vivarium*

*The One I Love*

*Mother!*

*Source Code*
  - I just rewatched Devs last year.  Such a great show that went totally under the radar
4. Caddo Lake! It is the closest thing to Dark you will  find.
5. TV shows:

FROM  
Yellowjackets  
Silo

FILMS: (I'll just do 3 cause theres thousands I can think of)

Hidden  
Carriers  
The Crazies


6. If you're open to TV series, the _tv series_ version of 12 Monkeys is phenomenal. It's the best and most mind-blowing time travel story out there. The movie version is completely different, and is so-so. So make sure you watch the TV show. 
7. Continuum.  It's a Canadian series.  Good time travel stuff.
8. The OA, though if you want a weaker series: The Rain. 
9. Probably the closest thing out there to Dark is Counterpart (2017-2019). It's incredible. I don't want to spoil any of it for you, so just look it up and find out as little as you need to to be convinced to see it.

## 5. Platonic love stories

**Score:** 22
**Body:** I’m interested in watching more movies that are about forming deep platonic bonds with someone. Could be any genre, some examples I like include Project Hail Mary, Zootopia, and I Love You Man. I just want the primary focus to be on forging a deep love for someone else non-romantically.

Thanks for your suggestions 🙂

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Rogue one 
2. Past Lives
3. Lord of the Rings
4. Thelma and Louise 
5. Swiss Army Man. The Little Princess. A Perfect World.
6. Lost in Translation
7. Frances Ha
8. The best is probably 84 Charring Cross Road
9. Planes, Trains, and Automobiles

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. As for as sports comedies go The Replacements starring Keanu Reeves and Gene Hackman is the one where I will stop and watch it every time it is on. Not only is it a great feelgood film but it is really well-balanced film.

**Score:** 623

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. “I know you're tired, I know you're hurting. And I wish I could say something that was classy and inspirational, but that just wouldn't be our style. Pain heals. Chicks dig scars. Glory lasts forever."
2. Jon Favreau steals the show in this one
  - I got the BALLLLLLLLLLL. go sit down danny. 
3. I always laugh at the cheerleader tryouts and the jail dance scene.
  - Is exotic a type of dance?

![gif](giphy|jw010VnXWVkUlPQ3xc)
4. Brooke Langton was so beautiful in that role
  - I thought she would have a bigger career.
5. He's wiry.
6. Gene Hackman in the role of an old school Tom Landry like coach fits like a glove. Plus John Madden and Pat Summerall in the booth is just legendary and soooo quotable.

I always love the last play when Falco launches the ball to the TE and the ball makes a sound effect like a plane taking off.
7. Pain heals. Chicks dig scars. Glory lasts forever.

## 2. What destroyed Kingsmen franchise?

**Score:** 507
**Body:** I really liked the first movie ,but second  Kingsmen  movie  is not very good ,plot is  forgettable  and why the hired Jeff Bridges , Halle Berry and Channing Tatum  for such small roles?

### Top 4 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The first was incredible.

Every one after was a letdown.  It was almost a lesson on how to destroy what could have been a great franchise.  Starting with killing off most of the remaining cast from the first movie, then bringing back to life somebody that had died in the first movie.
  - The 2nd was fun but was ruined by a few small things.

1.Killing off every single other Kingsman who survived movie one.

2. Killing off Merlin.

3. Bringing galahad back from the dead in the way they did.

3 could have been saved if it wasnt for 1 and 2.

I personally love Merlins death scene with the song. But it was still a bad choice.
2. The weird wwi spinoff that I can't distinguish in my head from the wonder woman wwi scenes. Clearly it was some other movie that they slapped "Kings Man" on the front of.
  - The post credit scene teasing Hitler like he's fucking Thanos still sends me to this day.
    - https://i.redd.it/wpdlqehq7vch1.gif

I liked this post credit scene with Hitler more.
      - ![gif](giphy|10z9elyRK06Qog)
        - Life Stinks!  That is actually one of my favorite Mel Brooks movies ever.

I enjoy his intellectual movies far more than his screwball ones.  Especially his dark comedies.  For those who have not seen it, think of "Trading Places".  But in the end, Dan Aykroyd decides to give up his riches and spends his wealth improving the lives of those that live in the slums he had intended to gentrify.
  - I actually liked that one. But maybe because it was interesting to see the reinterpretation of historical events. Like all the ways Rasputin was “killed” only to come back to life multiple ways until he finally downed in an icy river after surviving a gun shot to the head all distilled into one epic fight.

I think the 2nd one was pretty awful. They killed off Lancelot in the first few moments, undid a pivotal character death, and really didn’t evolve Egsy past his character growth from the first movie.
  - The silent trench fight was fricking dope tho. 
3. I’d say awful sequels. 

## 3. Which food/eating scene is your favorite?

**Score:** 462
**Body:** Aunt Meg’s steak & eggs with mashed potatoes and famous gravy in Twister.

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. ![gif](giphy|kMEcvKXTDDfQk)

Pulp Fiction - Big Kahuna Burger

## 4. Thoughts on Friendship?

**Score:** 391
**Body:** I bring this movie to the community because I’ve revisited a couple of times now and I know there’s still something there I haven’t quite grasped.

I am a big fan of Tim Robinson and his brand of humour. I especially like his unshakable confidence in absolute nonsense, escalating absurdity beyond limits with *extreme* emotional commitment. I was eager to see how it would translate to the big screen with fully fleshed out characters and storylines.

The first time I watched it, it was just a bridge too far, to be completely frank. Despite consistent laughs, the lack of *any* on-screen chemistry made for a deeply uncomfortable experience. I couldn’t understand why any of these characters wanted to spend any time with each other and the movie failed to connect, despite finding humour in the social awkwardness… I felt bathed in secondhand embarrassment by the end of it.

When I revisited it, I went in with the understanding that the lack of chemistry is a feature, not a flaw. The humour lies underneath the awkwardness. It made for a much more enjoyable experience as we watch someone completely incapable of communicating try to find acceptance, ignoring every social norm along the way. I felt closer to finding the heart of the film, but I was still left with nothing much more than a feeling akin to mortification.

The film’s director, Andrew DeYoung directed some of my favourite episodes of the AppleTV series *Widow’s Bay*. He collaborated with Tim Robinson on the HBO series *The Chair Company* which I found compelling, funny and unique and didn’t leave me with me with *as much* of an awkward feeling… so being a fan of both of their work, I know I will be revisiting the film again eventually and I’m just looking for some outside perspectives to help enhance my viewing experience.

What are your thoughts, positive or negative, on the movie *Friendship*?

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. This movie was better than the new marvel  that's out 

  - I said no spoilers!
  - I heard it’s NUTS
2. I have never sunk into a couch more than when he sticks the bar of soap in his mouth. 10/10. no notes. 
  - I get the *point* it was trying to make as a movie...but I also found it uncomfortable to the point that I never, ever want to watch it again.
3. One of my favourites. I would love to see more Tim headlining movies. And The Chair Company is great looking forward to season 2. 
4. I’ve always said that Paul Rudd is one of those actors where, if you put him in your movie, your movie is now a Paul Rudd movie, and the plot is secondary. In other words Ant-man is not a superhero movie starring Paul Rudd; it’s a Paul Rudd movie about a superhero.

This movie completely shattered that theory. This was a Tim Robinson movie all throughout. Tim Robinson doesn’t even have to do anything. Literally just putting the man on screen makes me crack the fuck up.

Funniest movie I’ve seen in a long time
5. # My life is nothing I thought it should be and everything I was worried it would become because for 1hr41mins I thought there was monsters on the world
6. So cringe, from the actor that does cringe the absolute best
7. We never should have left Afghanistan

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. Almodovar

**Score:** 58
**Body:** The more Almodovar moves I watch, the more I’m convinced he’s the best director in the world today, even better than the almighty Paul Thomas Anderson. He’s not everyone’s cup of tea, especially if you hate soap opera, but the way he combines melodrama, film noir, and dark comedy is ingenious.

Even at age 76, he’s still making subversive and transgressive movies and pushing the envelope. I’ve seen a lot of backlash about his 2002 masterpiece TALK TO HER—which is still highly ranked among the greatest films of the 21st century—from the younger generations (Puritans?) who claim the movie is pro-rape or something, but I’m gonna chalk that up to “media illiteracy.” I love that he never panders and stays productive

\[REPOST\]

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Talk to Her has one of the best screenplays of the century so far in my opinion. The way it draws parallels between the two relationships says all manner of things about sexuality, gender, obsession and self-delusion, and the use of artworks within the story (the song at the party, the silent film, the ballet performances) does a brilliant job of reflecting the experiences of the characters. What's even more brilliant is how Almodovar uses a twisty melodramatic plot almost as a way of distracting you from the film's intellectual muscle: you're so preoccupied with the narrative's peaks and troughs you don't realise how much the film has been exploring until it's over.
  - Agreed. It's fearless, and I find Almodovar's sureness of touch breathtaking, regarding a subject that most directors wouldn't dare go anywhere near. I love his use of Pina Bausch's extraordinary work in the film as well. I'd also recommend Wim Wenders' documentary 'Pina' if anyone wants to see more of this.
2. Pain And Glory in 2019 was a big surprise to me. I didn't expect that from Almodovar late career. This is based only on my own opinion, but with that film Almodovar cemented his status as one of the 'greats'.
  - Gorgeous movie. I love a lot of his prior ones (especially that 14-year run from *Live Flesh* through *The Skin I Live In*, where he just never missed, and some of the more anarchic early ones as well), but P&G became my favorite. 

This monologue is a beauty: "The films were projected on an enormous, whitewashed wall. I particularly remember the films in which there was water: waterfalls, beaches, the bottom of the sea, rivers or springs. Just hearing the sound of running water gave all the kids a tremendous desire to urinate, and we'd do it right there, on both sides of the screen. In the cinema of my childhood it always smells of piss, and jasmine, and of the summer breeze."
    - He got way, way better with age, dramatically so
  - I agree and it's one of my favorite movies of this century. It feels like the type that will get its due in the coming decades.
  - Almodovar is unpredictable. That’s basically his 8 1/2 (Fellini). Then he makes a Western short film out of nowhere 
3. I don't know about best director overall, 'cause that's an argument which turns into fanboy ranting very quickly, but by god, he's the best male director of women. Talk to Her is indeed a masterpiece, and could definitely be seen as an argument for empathy, but not for rape. 

In a society where some people would argue that empathy is the worst of the two, we need creators like Almodovar.
4. This post made me so happy. Just yesterday I stumbled upon a DVD copy of Volver in a flea market and had to have it even though my laptop can’t play it. His movies literally shaped my understanding of cinema and women. I would watch The Cannibalistic Councillor religiously in high school. But his recent works, I don’t find enjoyable honestly. After I’m So Excited, I felt more and more distant from his works, which is a shame. But yeah, I do agree that he is one of the bests.  
5. He's my favorite, hands down. I could watch *Talk to Her* or *The Skin I Live In* or *Matador* (so slept on) or *What Have I Done To Deserve This?* every night for the rest of my life and never get bored. Reflecting another comment here, there is no male artist in any medium that I have encountered who seems to understand what it is to navigate life in a feminized body. I too am fascinated by what I see as misreadings of his work, especially in regards to rape and sexual assault. Rather than minimize such things, he recognizes what a standard and constantly present part of womanhood (or anyone placed into a female/feminized positionality) it is. And good lord does that man know how to end a film.

  
I also recommend [his collection of short stories](https://www.ebar.com/story/152316) if you haven't read them--direct and indirect echoes of his cinematic work and compelling in their own regard as well.

## 2. What are some modern-ish Hollywood films that could be considered “poetic”?

**Score:** 29
**Body:** I was thinking about the trio Bresson, Tarkovsky and Bergman who are often referred to as “poetic” film makers. Then there are people like Antonioni, Jarmusch, Denis, Wong Kar Wai, Tsai Min Lang etc etc who have obvious poetic tendencies in a lot of work.

I suppose a lot depends on how you define “poetic” in the first place, and what Tarkovsky meant when he himself called Bresson and Bergman poetic.

Recently I am wondering what modern-ish US Hollywood films might have managed to slip through the cracks and be called “poetic”?

I feel like I might be able to make a case for “Gravity” (2013), (although given the director Cuaron it makes sense!) Soderberg’s “Solaris” (2002) possibly, although a bit heavy handed. Maybe Linklater’s “Boyhood” (2012)? None of these are really on the same level though. “It Follows”???

I’m scratching my head feeling like there must be a ton of films i am missing.

EDIT: I want to add a post about what I consider makes a film “poetic”. In a literary poem, the poet uses the basic tools of the written word (word choice, sentence structure, metre, rhyme, line breaks, page design, metaphor etc etc) to create a highly structured work on the page that triggers a much deeper range of emotion and visual imagery than what is simply there on the page. In film, we have Bresson using the basic tools of cinema (camera and lighting, cassette recorder, actor, setting) to create a single work that is intended to be seen as a whole, and reflected on only after the whole piece is finished. Tarkovsky was inspired by this methodolgy (and other things of course, like his father). Are there any modern Hollywood films (or American Indies) that mimic this? Highly unlikely, however there must be films that touch on it, or have film makers in the industry that allowed some of this mentality to filter through. Of course, we can argue what “poetic” actually means and if it is any of the above at all!

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. You have to differentiate between "Hollywood" and "American" because most poetic American films will be produced independently of Hollywood - like Linklater. David Lynch and Terrence Malick are two of our most defining poetic filmmakers, but they're not Hollywood. Even those with distinctive styles like Wes Anderson and the Coen Brothers are largely independent. A film like Solaris (Fox) or Gravity (Warner Bros) is a rare exception usually given to a filmmaker that made a studio a ton of money, but Hollywood doesn't sustain filmmakers like that for very long without demanding they do something more commercial.
2. Assassination of Jesse James has a lot of meditative moments and plays with time and memory in a way that feels very poetic. Not my favorite movie but it fits the bill. Also James Gray has a way with cinema that I find poetic. Ad Astra, despite the lunacy of certain moments, also has sequences that felt genuinely out of body
3. Malick, but I'm not sure you'd class him as Hollywood. Michael Mann mostly fits (Thief, Heat, Miami Vice, Mohicans), Scorcese sometimes (nearly all his films have poetic moments, but Silence is the only recent one start to finish). Shrader for sure, but again, not necessarily Hollywood. Most recently I'd include Garland.
4. Kristen Stewart's The Chronology of Water (2025) was VERY poetic in the Tarkovsky-like sense.

Leaving the more directly pictorial aspect: I somewhat feel that Uncut Gems (2019) was poetic (though a very different, percussive poem). I feel like some Cassavetes' and Altman films could be quite poetic. Blade Runner (1982) had many poetic elements. Sorcerer (1977) poetic. Kelly Reichardt poetic.
5. Tree of Life by Terrence Malick, and all of his subsequent films are incredibly poetic, some might say too poetic. However, post Tree of Life I believe he is not considered 'Hollywood' despite using Hollywood actors frequently. 
6. In what way is Gravity (2013) poetic cinema? I think it’s well made, but Cuarón himself even has said he considers it more of a commercial studio film instead of something more artistic. Roma could be considered a poetic film though, imo
7. *May December, Nickel Boys, The Lost Daughter, The Power of the Dog, C’mon C’mon, The Card Counter, First Reformed, Passing, Tragedy of Macbeth, Tar, Sing Sing*

Mostly new stuff I just pulled from my top lists of the last few years. Also *The Underground Railroad*, although it’s a limited series.
  - This is a great list. I want to really reinforce *C'mon C'mon*, a film that left me in tears both times I watched it. 

I'll add a bunch more (mostly indie, but American) films: *A Girl Walks Home Alone at Night*, *Beginners*, *Carol* (and *Safe* too), *Chop Shop*, *Columbus*, *The Future*, *George Washington*, *Lingua Franca*, *Lost in Translation*, *Minari*, *Past Lives*, *Pig* and *The Virgin Suicides*.

And another Jane Campion, maybe not modern'ish though: *The Piano*.

Hmm, does *Cloud Atlas* count? If *Gravity* does, I'd say so.

And while it's not American, I simply cannot not mention *Petite Maman*.

Also OP, you can't catalog poetic directors and not mention Theo Angelopoulos. https://old.reddit.com/r/TrueFilm/comments/1r7nmu9/all_the_films_of_theo_angelopoulos_are_now/
8. I can only think that Patterson fits the mold, american book poetry adapted with the same feeling, starring Adam Driver and by a recognizable american director, Gus Van Sant. It's not from the 2020's but it's one of the best representation of poetry on screen I've watched in a long time. For something outside the U.S. I'll recommend A Poet, colombian movie with a great script and protagonist. 
  - That one is directed by Jim Jarmusch. Good pick!

## 3. What Pauline Kael, F.R. Leavis, and Northrop Frye can teach us about critical dogmatism

**Score:** 14
**Body:** [This essay](https://quillette.com/2026/07/12/strong-opinions-pauline-kael-fr-leavis-northrop-frye/) uses Pauline Kael's take-no-prisoners film criticism and F.R. Leavis's rigid "Great Tradition" as cautionary examples of critics who issue verdicts rather than illuminate, and holds up Northrop Frye's more expansive, non-judgmental framework as the alternative. Curious what this sub thinks: is a critic's real job to render verdicts (Kael, Leavis), or to map and illuminate without ruling things in or out (Frye)? And can aesthetic judgment ever really be separated from moral judgment, as the piece struggles with re: *Dirty Harry* and Bill T.

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. At the end of the day film reviews in the New Yorker have a service journalism component as well as a cultural criticism component.   Kael might have put on airs and pretended to be above Ebert and his thumbs, but she fundamentally wrote in a fancypants list of things to do in New York over the weekend.  It’s silly to fault her for being judgmental.  The real question is the quality of her judgment.

I’m with team Adler, late stage Keal is “**jarringly, piece by piece, line by line, and without interruption, worthless”**
2. >is a critic's real job to render verdicts (Kael, Leavis), or to map and illuminate without ruling things in or out (Frye)?

Certainly both, and I'd expand criticism to be more than that as well.

I've mentioned here before, a few times, that I am a big fan of Roger Ebert, who of course cited Kael as a major influence. But while Roger is famous for his negative reviews, I've often found him much more engaging when he's illuminating the work he loves--or even just elements he loves about a movie he doesn't, overall. 

I think what Ebert showed me about criticism, and what I've since sought out not just in film criticism but in literary criticism as well, is that the critic's greatest strength is being able to give voice to their enthusiasm toward the art, and to give their readers ways to find their own enthusiasm along with it. When I read his review of *Joe vs. The Volcano*, and he describes that joyful feeling in the opening scenes of realizing that, in an industry where most movies are pieces of other movies stitched together, here was a movie *he'd never seen before*, I know exactly what he means, and I find those experiences in my own memories. Similarly, when he notes some bit of sentiment that catches him just right, or some visual effect that lends the film a texture that he finds remarkable, he's giving voice to enthusiasm, and helping others to find their own enthusiasm as well, rather than simply telling them how good this specific thing is, or what it accomplishes.

When I read a good critic, I don't feel that I'm didactically being told about the art; I feel like I'm being invited into the conversation about the art, both in terms of this specific movie or book or what have you, but also in the broader sense of the medium.
3. I think Pauline Kael had some of the worst takes of all time, but she was an extremely good writer and she was very good at what she did. She had a point about the Hollywood blockbuster, and if anything, I think she underestimated how bad it was going to get. I disagree with her stance on auteur theory, and I disagree with her belief that films need to elicit visceral reactions in order to be powerful or good. But I respect her convictions, and honestly, it's not her fault that people listened to her and took her opinion as gospel. If anything, it's a testament to how good she really was.

As far as the job of the critic to issue verdicts, I absolutely agree that is what's expected of them. The average person doesn't have the time to see 300 movies a year, they need someone to tell them which things are worth watching and which don't deserve your time. It's up to the reader of those reviews to decide whether the reviewer's opinion is worth respecting.
4. I admired Kael for her brashness, and always felt she explained her verdicts well. I did not often agree with her, but I felt she taught me a different perspective on many titles. I do whole-heartedly agree with her enthusiam for 1978's Invasion of the Body Snatchers.
5. Kael was so wrong so many times it became an unfunny joke. Her takes were silly (Kubrick bad, DePalma good), but it was her imperious tone that truly offended. She seemed to think her work was as important as Scorsese’s. Now she’s forgotten except as an example of what not to do. Fitting. 
  - I agree with you about her takes being maddingly shit, but it's pretty crazy to imply Pauline Kael is forgotten when she's probably the 3rd or 4th most recognizable film critic to ever live. She's not just remembered as an example of what not to do; considering how many critics followed in her footsteps desperate to emulate her, I would say the opposite.
  - The implication that DePalma being good is a silly take here is what’s most offensive

## 4. What Have You Been Watching? (Week of (July 12, 2026)

**Score:** 10
**Body:**  Please don't downvote opinions. Only downvote comments that don't contribute anything. Check out the [WHYBW archives](https://www.reddit.com/r/TrueFilm/wiki/index#wiki_what_have_you_been_watching.3F).

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I rewatched Natural Born Killers after many years. I once considered it a masterpiece, and while I wouldn't rate it nearly so highly today (and I can better understand where its detractors are coming from), I still think it's very impressive.

This is actually the film that first shook me awake to cinema when I was an adolescent, and I suspect I'm not alone in that. The wild montage-style editing and the mishmash of different stocks, formats, filters, and techniques was revelatory—I'd never seen anything like that before.

This time around, I was especially struck by the performances. Both leads are incandescent—career bests, I would say—and all three supporting performances are excellent as well.

I don't think the film is particularly well thought of today, but it's still a must-see in my view.
2. **The Twilight Saga: Breaking Dawn — Part 2 (2012, Bill Condon)** — I’m not sure whether *Breaking Dawn — Part 2* is the least bad *Twilight* movie, or the worst of them all; I guess the fairest analysis is that it’s neither. On the one hand, this is certainly the best-shot and best-looking movie in the saga; on a purely technical level, the series seems to have finally risen to the level of unremarkable competence. And writing-wise, the movie has a story that sort-of manages to function as an actual plot, which is an unusual step forward for this series. On the other hand, while none of the lead actors were any good in any of these movies, this is the first time that everyone down to the smallest bit actors seems to have just completely given up. Even Billy Burke — consistently one of the better performers in the previous movies, and probably still the least-awful even here — can’t find a way to make lines like “I just watched a kid I’ve known his entire life turn into a very large dog” sound even remotely natural, while Michael Sheen, whose brief appearance in *New Moon* was maybe the only unironically good part of the whole series, has now descended completely into a parody of himself. Moreover, this is undoubtedly the most morally repulsive movie in the whole saga. While the whole franchise has always implicitly treated Bella as the only person whose well-being matters, and that everyone else exists solely to suck up to her, the previous movies never rubbed the audience’s faces in it quite as intensely as they do here.

**START OF SPOILERS**

Obviously, the big thing everyone knocks this movie for, in terms of offensive themes, is Jacob imprinting on Bella’s newborn daughter. (The imprinting itself actually happens at the end of the *Part 1* — and in the books, the general concept of child imprinting was established already in *Eclipse* — but this is where it’s most central to the story.) And while that would be enough to make this the most appalling movie in the series by itself, it isn’t helped by the fact that Bella actually *does* try to object to it… entirely on the grounds of how it affects *her*; her sole stated reason for being mad about Jacob wanting to groom her *infant* daughter is that she hates the thought of Jacob getting to know Renesmee before she does. This objection having been raised and quickly dismissed, nobody else takes any issue with the situation for the entire rest of the movie. Moreover, if one had only seen the previous movies in the series, one might reasonably have assumed that the Cullens were considered better than other vampires *because* they don’t eat people. *Breaking Dawn — Part 2* clarifies that this is merely incidental, and that the real distinction between a good vampire and an evil one is solely and entirely based on how willing they are to kiss Bella’s ass. The friends whom the Cullens call on in their fight against the Volturi — not uneasy allies, but actual longtime friends of the family — include numerous vampires just as brutal as any of the villains seen previously, one of whom is literally in the middle of committing a murder as they find him, but they’re the good guys now because there’s no longer any worry that they’ll kill Bella *specifically*.

**END OF SPOILERS**

I suppose it is somewhat of an achievement, given how much worse the books got with each instalment, that all but one of the *Twilight* movies are about the same level of “quality”. I guess if I had to place *Part 2* against the other movies in a ranking, I’d probably place it in the middle of the pack — worse than the first movie and *Eclipse*, not as bad as *Part 1*, around tied with or maybe slightly worse than *New Moon* — but, other than the staggering awfulness of *Breaking Dawn — Part 1*, the differences are small enough that I’d say they all earned about the same final rating. **3/10**

**West Side Story (2021, Steven Spielberg)** — Spielberg’s version of *West Side Story* is pretty good, but it could have been a lot better if it had been done more as a loose homage, rather than an outright adaptation or remake. The biggest changes that Spielberg makes to the traditional story are (a) giving Tony more of a fleshed-out character, turning him into more of a tragic antihero rather than a vaguely-defined symbol of innocence, and (b) giving the entire thing a darker, grimier aesthetic that makes the threat of the gangs feel edgier and more realistic. Both of these are, in themselves, good ideas that could have led to an even better movie than the classic 1961 version… if not for the fact that they still keep all of the original song-and-dance numbers, rather than writing a new set of songs to fit the new mood or just making it a non-musical entirely. While some might argue that the frequently-awkward corniness of Sondheim’s lyricism was a weak point of the story from the start, I’d say that it worked oddly well in the original, because it fit with the vibe of a tragedy about lost kids who don’t fully understand the ramifications of their actions. Here, with all of the main characters being given more of a jaded and cynical edge from the start, songs like “Something Good” or “Tonight” here become so out-of-place as to be completely unworkable, even with the numbers being staged and directed as stunningly as they are here. Giving this a modest recommendation. **7/10**

**Harry Potter and the Prisoner of Azkaban (2004, Alfonso Cuarón) — re-watch** — I like the *Harry Potter* movies, but I don’t like them nearly as much as I wish I did. Sometimes I’ve wondered if loving the books so much has made it too hard for me to appreciate the movies on their own merits, but after trying to revisit *Prisoner of Azkaban* — usually considered the best movie in the series — with as open a mind as I can, I’m now confident that they really just aren’t that good.

Viewed purely as a spectacle, the movie version of *Prisoner of Azkaban* works. Other than the godawful CGI of Lupin’s transformations, the effects work and production design do a great job of bringing the Wizarding World to life as a compelling place, and that does carry the movie through a lot of its weaker elements. Viewed as a movie about a story and a group of characters, it’s… like… *fine*. There are none of the profound, movie-killing flaws that one finds in something like a *Transformers* movie, or even in the MCU. But it’s not really anything to write home about, either.

I know this is not a particularly original observation, but by far the biggest thing that drags this movie down — both as an adaptation and as a story in its own right — is the baffling character assassination of Ron Weasley. Granted, Ron had plenty of shitty moments in the books, but they usually served a purpose within the story and in fleshing out his character; and it was counterbalanced by both Harry and Hermione getting plenty of shitty moments of their own. In the movies, the writers seem to just throw a moment of Ron being a dick into every scene where they can find an opportunity, and then just never acknowledge it or try to do anything with it. Meanwhile, almost every unflattering moment Harry and Hermione had in the book has been removed, so that one of the most iconic friendship dynamics in modern pop-culture is reduced to “two blandly perfect heroes have a douchebag tagging along with them for some reason”.

Story-wise, the movie is… well, as I said, it’s fine. It’s fun and works well as long as you don’t think too hard about it. But the more attention you pay to the details of what’s going on, the more minor elements there are that just don’t work.

**START OF SPOILERS**

For instance: the opening scene of the movie has Harry, in his bedroom, using a magic light from his wand to read a letter in the dark, rather than the normal flashlight that he used in the book. Nothing about the scene suggests that this is something strange or dangerous. But after the inflation of Aunt Marge, Harry claims — as in the books — that using magic while at Privet Drive is a serious crime, so what the hell was that about? Or: both Snape and Lupin refer to the Marauder’s Map as part of Lupin’s “area of expertise”, and Sirius reacts to the suggestion of the Map being wrong as though it’s a personal attack on him. If you’ve read the book, you know this is foreshadowing the reveal that Lupin and Sirius were among those who made the map in the first place… but the movie removes the actual reveal itself, so that these lines just seem like pointless *non sequitur*s. And wow, what an amazing coincidence that, while wandering around in his Invisibility Cloak, Harry should just happen to overhear a massive info dump that gives him all of the information about Sirius Black and Peter Pettigrew needed for him to move the story forward. In the book… okay, that part actually was just as stupid in the book, but it’s not like the movie made it any *better*.

**END OF SPOILERS**

Again, any one of these holes, by itself, would be pretty minor and easy to overlook. But at some point, minor issues do add up. Giving this a modest recommendation. **7/10**

**Spirited Away (2001, Hayao Miyazaki) — re-watch** — The movie that introduced me to Miyazaki, and I still think it’s probably the best work of his career. Also probably the best filmed version of *Through the Looking-Glass* that we’re ever going to get. **10/10**
  - **The Adventures of Robin Hood (1938, Michael Curtiz) — re-watch** — Why does this movie hold up so well? In the core ingredients of its story — the roguish hero, the maiden love, the treacherous prince, and so forth — it’s about as broad and basic as it gets. And yet, so few of the countless action-adventure movies that have come out since, including those based on the same traditional legends, have anything like the enduring appeal that it does. It’s an easy answer, but I think a lot of it really does just come down to how magnetic Errol Flynn’s portrayal of Robin Hood is. Flynn’s range as an actor does seem a bit limited here (I am not familiar enough with his work to say if this is true in general), but his charisma and star quality are hard to match. Keeping this as a **9/10**.

**Across the Universe (2007, Julie Taymor)** — This may seem an odd way to start a review, but I really don’t understand my own reaction to *Across the Universe*. It’s a godawful movie by almost every measure I can think of, with its near-nonexistent plot, uniformly uninteresting and one-dimensional characters, mediocre performances, and frequently weirdly tacky and amateur-looking visuals. About the only thing it has going for it is the sheer density of classic Beatles songs that Taymor has managed to work in, but even that is undermined by how awkwardly most of them are crowbarred. (And playing a song about how sometimes you just need to let troubles be and not respond to them over a scene of a Civil Rights protest is… certainly a choice.) And yet, for some strange reason, I found myself drawn to it; and it can’t just be due to the quality of the songs, because that didn’t stop me from hating *Help!*. I guess I just admire the sheer commitment the movie has to forcing in every kind of Beatles reference the creators could think of, no matter how little sense it makes or how pointlessly it drags the movie out. It would be silly to give it a positive rating on that ground, though, but I also don’t feel right giving it a bad one, so I’ll just leave this as **Not sure how to rate**.

**Movie of the week:** *Spirited Away*
3. **City of God (2002, dir. Fernando Meirelles)**

Vibrant, dizzying film. Meirelles's style is slick and arresting, clashing nicely with a structure that cris-crosses time and space and weaves together a film that tells about seven or eight stories but never loses plot threads or feels like it's tripping over itself. Everything gets its time to develop, and the result is a story that feels so much bigger than its two hour runtime.  I thought the focus on children and how they get trapped in the violent, all-too-short lives of slum gangsters was effective. Few of these characters are older than eighteen, but it's easy to completely forget that when they're forced to raise themselves amidst street wars and extreme poverty. It was a more effective way to generate sympathy for society's downtrodden than merely showing whimpering street orphans over emotional violin solos. In spite of its serious subject matter and social commentary, the energy of the cast and the editing keep City of God fairly entertaining and engaging.

**Harakiri (1962, dir. Masaki Kobayashi)**

Harakiri is the highest-rated movie on Letterboxd, and it's not hard to see why. Harakiri plays with structure and genre to tell a meaningful, evocative story about the very concept of honor. It dishes out information in a very precise, deliberate way, and each reveal is more meaningful than the one that came before. Its central arguments revolve around a deconstruction of samurai honor, arguing that such a concept is ultimately arbitrary and only as meaningful as the people who claim to adhere to it. The narrative payoffs are as satisfying as they are ambiguous, ending the story but not the conversation. Harakiri's influences on the anti-westerns of the seventies and beyond are clear, and the genre - and global cinema as a whole - is better off for it.

**The Heart of Texas Ryan (1917, dir. EA Martin)**

The Heart of Texas Ryan is a western about a person named Texas. Any writer worth their salt would use a character with such an on-the-nose name to make a meaningful comment about the state of Texas, effectively using the character as an allegorical figure. What did screenwriter Martin do? Not much, but I don't think he could have done much. From what I can tell, all surviving prints of The Heart of Texas Ryan come from a 1920s rerelease that stitched together parts of earlier westerns starring Tom Mix. As a result, the story of The Heart of Texas Ryan is all over the place at worst and barebones at best. It's hard to tell what's original and what's reused, but from what I can tell Martin did little more than use the character Texas Ryan to wring his hands about a modernizing American west and the threat Latin American and/or Mexican bandits posed to white Texans. The principal character bringing these themes to life is Mix's cowpuncher/gunfighter Single Shot Parker, hopelessly in love with a photograph of the titular Texas Ryan who can only win her reciprocation by routing the Latinos out of Texas over and over again. It's all as lousy and nasty as you can imagine.

**Begotten (1989, dir. E. Elias Merhige)**

The heavy mythological allusions and blunt, grainy visual style preclude this from being just a cheap Eraserhead imitation, but Begotten is still an ostensibly hollow art film. Aside from some mildly interesting/disturbing visuals, there isn't a lot that sticks with you. The high black-and-white contrast and minimalistic soundscape work with the slow pacing at first to build a sense of unease, but eventually you just get bored. I like the mythological aspect and the commentary on organized religion, but I don't think there's enough to justify an already brisk seventy-minute runtime.

**Schindler's List (1993, dir. Steven Spielberg)**

I think, after twenty-odd years of a mostly okay-to-good output from Spielberg, it's easy to underestimate the guy, but when he's great he earns his spot in the cinema hall of fame. That's not to say that I had low expectations for Schindler's List, but it did still exceed them. It's a heart-wrenchingly personal film, and you feel that in every frame. He channels cultural trauma and exceptional directorial skill into a movie that demolishes the standards of your typical Spielberg movie, creating something unflinching but still deeply human. The violence feels authentic, which makes the appeals to emotion feel all the more real. I can easily imagine a version of the scene where Schindler laments the fact that he could have saved more people that pushes things a little too far into sentimentalism, but after three hours of impenetrable misery I think that much emotion is earned.

A lot of this is helped, of course, by Schindler himself. The choice to focus on a non-Jewish opportunistic industrialist who's also a member of the Nazi party was a fascinating one, and I love how bluntly Spielberg writes him. Schindler is seldom a likeable person until the third act. He's always schmoozing with Nazis, always indifferent with his Jewish workers, always looking out for the bottom line. There's no rationalization on Spielberg's part, no part where he thinks "If they think I'm a die-hard Nazi, no one will suspect a thing!" He's not even immediately convinced to look out for the Jews' welfare when he witnesses the liquidation of the Krakow ghetto. He can look heartlessness and pure evil in the eye and continue on the way he's always been. In other words, he's a totally ordinary person. Ever since the fall of the Weimar Republic, people in Western democracies have been obsessed with resisting fascism and keeping an eye out for it if and when it arises in their own backyards, but I'd wager we'd all be like Schindler, looking out for ourselves until it becomes emotionally inconvenient to do so, typically at a point where far too many people have already died. This is what makes Schindler's arc so resonant. It's gradual and organic, nurturing inner goodness instead of foisting it onto a person who was never capable of it in the first place. We should all hope to be Oskar Schindler by the time we pay our last debt.

Liam Neeson portrays him magnificently, and everyone else can stand toe-to-toe with him. I'd heard a lot about Ralph Feinnes' character (wish he was in the movie more, but I guess it makes sense if the real-life camp did dissolve when it did), but not about Ben Kingsley's, who has about as much screentime as Oskar Schindler. He could easily have been the protagonist of his own story, and the three-hour runtime gives him plenty of opportunities to demonstrate his agency within Schindler's. He's not going to obey the whims of a German messiah and act on his own accord, either as a lone agent or as Schindler's partner in crime. I loved that, and it made the focus on a man as initially unheroic as Schindler all the more fascinating.

The other strengths are almost self-evident: the cinematography, the editing, the innovative use of color, the music, the direction. If there's a reason so much of Spielberg's 21st century output is so lacking, maybe it's because he left so much on screen with this one. I'm not going to return to this as much as Jurassic Park or Indiana Jones, but even I have to wonder how someone could hope to top this.

**Dogtooth (2009, dir. Yorgos Lanthimos)**

Dogtooth is not a movie that holds your hand. It frankly depicts the life of a repressed family and expects you to understand what's going on. I appreciate this lack of traditional exposition, but beyond a critique of overzealous parents who shelter children to their detriment, I struggle to extract much meaning. A lot of the scenes come off as shocking and bizarre for the sake of being shocking and bizarre, not really building up to anything but reiterating an earlier theme. The pacing suffers as a result, and after an hour or so I was ready for it to end. I'm glad, though, that Lanthimos managed to catch the attention of other production studios and has made better, more elaborate, more complex, more visually interesting films.
4. **Gail Daughtry and the Celebrity Sex Pass (2026, David Wain)**

I got to see a special preview screening of this one and I thought it was hilarious! Directed by David Wain, known for Wet Hot American Summer. Follows a hall pass gone wrong for engaged couple Gail and Tom. Lots of laughs for this one! **6.5/10**

**The Long Walk (2025, Francis Lawrence)**

Having never read the Stephen King novel (one of his first?) I went in with no expectations. Interesting if not ridiculous premise, it was just okay for me. Could’ve shaved off 20 minutes maybe. **5.5/10**

**Ready or Not 2: Here I Come (2026, Matt Bettinelli-Olpin, Tyler Gillett)**

I saw some negative reviews on some of the horror subs so was thinking this sequel might be a bust, but I ended up really enjoying it! Great balance of humor and action, Samara Weaving is awesome as the lead. Fun popcorn movie. **6.5/10**
5. Last night I saw Paris, Texas (1984, Wim Wenders) and Brokeback Mountain (2003, Ang Lee).

I don’t remember the last time I was so completely absorbed in a movie as Paris, Texas. The performances, the writing, but especially the lighting just drew me in in a way very few movies ever do. 

Brokeback Mountain is one of the most beautiful and heartbreaking movies I’ve ever seen. I cried in the theater and I cried again when I got home, but for as long and devastating as it is I almost want to watch it again today. We really lost a real one in Heath Ledger.

## 5. The Conformist (1970)

**Score:** 8
**Body:** Basically, I'm looking for some writing/scholarship/etc about this film. Most of the stuff I can find approaches it from a thematic angle, but I'm more interested in the visual and stylistic aspects of the film: there are so many formal choices made by Bertolucci and co. that intrigue me but that aren't particularly easy to parse, so I'd be grateful if anyone knows where I can find pieces that address the motivation behind the film's aesthetic.

### Top 3 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I think it's important to think of The Conformist as a film of its moment, 1970, even though the narrative is set 30-40 years prior. The political content -- which informs the structure and style of the film -- had direct relevance to the time of the film's making. Do a search on Anni di Piombo.
2. I'm pretty sure there are some good extras on the Criterion Channel. Otherwise, grab a book. There's a collection of Bertolucci interviews that I'm sure would be beneficial to check out.
3. Here is a [link](https://www.heise.de/tests/Tiefgruendige-Faschismus-Analyse-Bertoluccis-Der-Konformist-im-4K-Heimkinotest-11349461.html?giftToken=26a99710-d5ef-47ab-bf20-5dfd12e22db9) to a review of the newly released 4K-Edition in german. Check it out, maybe with the help of a translating tool
